entry.title: Early Numbering With Number Range Object In RAP
entry.content:
Introduction:

In most business applications, key fields such as Purchase Order number, Sales Order number, or Document ID are automatically generated using a number range object. This ensures uniqueness, consistency, and compliance with business standards.

In the SAP RESTful ABAP Programming Model (RAP), handling number generation requires a clear understanding of the framework lifecycle. Unlike classical ABAP, where numbers are typically generated during SAVE or in user exits, RAP introduces structured phases like Early Numbering and Late Numbering.

Early Numbering is used when the document number must be generated at the time of CREATE itself, even before the data is saved to the database.

Solution Overview:

In this blog, we will explore how to implement Early Numbering using a Number Range Object in RAP, including:

  • Creating and configuring a custom Number Range Object

  • Implementing EARLYNUMBERING_CREATE in the Behavior Implementation

  • Properly updating mapped entities using %control

  • Avoiding common mistakes like type mismatch and value reset issues

Root Entity:

@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED
@EndUserText.label: 'interface view for purchase header'
@Metadata.ignorePropagatedAnnotations: true
define root view entity zr_trv_etag as select from zpur_hdr
{
key ebeln as Ebeln,
bukrs as Bukrs,
bstyp as Bstyp,
bsart as Bsart  ,
last_changed_at as lastchangedat  
}

Projection View:

@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED
@EndUserText.label: 'purchase header consumption view'
@Metadata.ignorePropagatedAnnotations: true
define root view entity zc_pur_hdr as projection on zr_trv_etag
{
@UI.facet: [{ type: #IDENTIFICATION_REFERENCE, label: 'Purchase Header' }]
@UI.lineItem: [{ position: 10 }]
@UI.identification: [{ position: 10 }]
    key Ebeln,
@UI.lineItem: [{ position: 20 }]
@UI.identification: [{ position: 20 }]

    Bukrs,
    @ui.lineItem: [{ position: 30 }]
@UI.identification: [{ position: 30 }]
    
    Bstyp,
    @ui.lineItem: [{ position: 40 }]
@UI.identification: [{ position: 40 }]
    
    Bsart
}

Interface Behavior Definition & Define Early Numbering:

managed implementation in class zbp_r_trv_etag unique;
strict ( 2 );
with draft;
define behavior for zr_trv_etag alias i_purhdr
persistent table ZPUR_HDR
draft table zdpurhdr
lock master total etag lastchangedat
authorization master ( instance )
early numbering
//etag master <field_name>
{
  create;
  update;
  delete;
  field(readonly) Ebeln;
  draft action Activate ;
  draft action Discard;
  draft action Edit;
  draft action Resume;
  draft determine action Prepare;
  mapping for ZPUR_HDR{

  Ebeln = ebeln;
  Bukrs = bukrs;
  Bsart = bsart;
  Bstyp = bstyp;
  }
}

Early Numbering Implementation:

METHOD EARLYNUMBERING_CREATE.

   TRY.

    cl_numberrange_runtime=>number_get(
      EXPORTING
        nr_range_nr       = '01'
        object            = 'ZPUR_DOC_N'
        quantity          = 1
      IMPORTING
        number            = data(lv_pur_id)
        returncode        = DATA(lv_subrc)
        returned_quantity = DATA(lv_total_quantity)
    ).
  CATCH cx_nr_object_not_found.
  CATCH cx_number_ranges.
ENDTRY.

loop at entities into data(wa_entity) .
if WA_ENTITY-Ebeln is INITIAL.
    wa_entity-Ebeln = lv_pur_id+10(10).

    append value #( %cid     = wa_entity-%cid
                    %key     = wa_entity-%key
                    %is_draft = wa_entity-%is_draft )
      to mapped-I_PURHDR.

else.

    append value #( %cid     = wa_entity-%cid
                    %key     = wa_entity-%key
                    %is_draft = wa_entity-%is_draft )
      to mapped-I_PURHDR.
      ENDIF.
ENDLOOP.

  ENDMETHOD.

Number Range Object Creation:

  • tcode : SNRO

  • give number range object.

 

 

 

Service Definition:

 

Service Binding:

 

Preview:

Create:

 

 

Conclusion:

Early Numbering in RAP allows us to generate document numbers at the time of creation itself using a Number Range Object. This is useful when the key field must be available immediately on the UI or required for draft processing. By correctly implementing the EARLYNUMBERING_CREATE method and updating the mapped entity with %control, we can ensure the generated number is properly assigned and saved. Understanding this concept helps developers avoid common mistakes and build stable, standard-compliant RAP applications.

Check Out My Other Blogs:

Topic1 :  CALL BAPI IN CUSTOM ENTITY THROUGH RAP
            Link :      CALL BAPI IN CUSTOM ENTITY THROUGH RAP
                  Topic2 :  Hide/Show Field In UI Dynamically Through Virtual Elements In RAP
            Link :       Hide/Show Field In UI Dynamically Through Virtual Elements In RAP
                         Topic3 :  RAP BASED ADOBEFORM WITH CUSTOM ACTION and Annotations
            Link :      RAP BASED ADOBEFORM WITH CUSTOM ACTION and Annotations
                        Topic4 :  Date Hierarchy Using Tree ALV(FM & OOPS )
            Link :      Date Hierarchy Using Tree ALV(FM & OOPS)
                         Topic5 :  Convert XML File to Internal Table And Download Into CSV Format In ABAP
            Link :       Convert XML File to Internal Table And Download Into CSV Format In ABAP
                         Topic6 :  Dynamic Font Styling in Adobe Forms (JavaScript)
            Link :       Dynamic Font Styling in Adobe Forms (JavaScript)
                        Topic7 :   Previewing and Downloading Adobe Forms Made Easy with ABAP
            Link :       Previewing and Downloading Adobe Forms Made Easy with ABAP

entry.title: Re: Deficit of Stck w.vend.unr./rev X PC XXX IN PO Subcon...
entry.content:
Good day @AngelT 

Thank you for your question.

Based on your description, it is not a tolerance setting issue but a subcontracting posting logic. From your screenshots and details, the system lets you enter a GR quantity on the screen, during check, it validates the 543 movement logic and if vendor special stock is insufficient (which aligns with your case), it raises the deficit message and blocks full posting. So, it is consistent with error behaviour around M7021 for subcontracting components.

Consult the following references for more details:

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3676051

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/2525797

So, the system might have looked like it allowed you, however, the key point is:

  • The UI can accept typed quantity first
  • The hard stock validation will then happen when you execute. That means the final posting is restricted by component availability.

Also note that, your settings in PO/info record is responsible for controlling PO quantity tolerance, not whether component stock exists for subcontracting consumption. So, those settings do not override component deficit checks.

Question: Is this a CBC switch?

Answer: For the description you provided, this is not typically a CBC switch to bypass component availability. It has to do with core subcontracting goods movement validation when you post.

 

Here is what you should check in your system:

Confirm component requirement for GR quantity in your Post Goods Receipts for Purchasing Document app.

In your Manage Stock app, confirm vendor special stock and check component material stock in your relevant plant and storage location.

Then in your Manage Purchase Orders app, consult the PO history and verify how much was already posted and corresponding consumption of the component.

You can retest with your scenario with a quantity that is equal to the feasible component stock.

In summary:

  • The system checks entry first, then strict component stock is checked during posting, so it is working as intended.
  • Tolerance settings are not the controlling factor for subcontracting component deficits.

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: From Cloud Foundry to Kyma on SAP BTP: 5 Essential Migration Patterns
entry.content:
From Cloud Foundry to Kyma on SAP BTP: 5 Essential Migration Patterns

Introduction

This blog covers five core patterns you'll need when moving from CF to Kyma, with working examples you can test on a BTP Trial Kyma cluster. Each pattern is presented as a direct comparison: here's how you did it in Cloud Foundry, here's how you do it in Kyma. The code examples are complete and tested—you can copy them into your own Kyma environment and see them work. By the end, you'll have a practical understanding of the migration path and reference code you can adapt for your own applications.

What you'll learn:

  1. Basic deployment with APIRules (Istio-based routing)
  2. Service bindings using Kubernetes-native patterns
  3. Pre-runtime configuration with init containers
  4. Credential Store integration for secrets management
  5. Destination Service integration from Kyma

Prerequisites

Before starting, you'll need:

  • SAP BTP Trial account with Kyma environment enabled
  • kubectl CLI installed locally
  • Basic familiarity with Kubernetes concepts

Setup: Download Your Kubeconfig

First, let's get connected to your Kyma cluster:

# Create directory structure
mkdir -p ~/kyma-tutorials
cd ~/kyma-tutorials

# Download kubeconfig from BTP Cockpit
# Navigate to: Subaccount → Kyma Environment → Download Kubeconfig
# Save to ~/.kube/config-kyma-trial

# Set kubeconfig
export KUBECONFIG=~/.kube/config-kyma-trial

# Verify connection
kubectl cluster-info

Critical first step: Enable Istio sidecar injection on your namespace. Kyma requires this for external routing to work:

# Create namespace
kubectl create namespace demo-app

# Enable Istio injection
kubectl label namespace demo-app istio-injection=enabled

# Set as default
kubectl config set-context --current --namespace=demo-app

Pattern 1: Deployment and Routing with APIRules

CF Pattern:

cf push myapp

Kyma Pattern: In Kyma, you need three resources: Deployment, Service, and APIRule.

apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: hello-kyma
  namespace: demo-app
spec:
  replicas: 1
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: hello-kyma
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: hello-kyma
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: hello
        image: hashicorp/http-echo:latest
        args:
          - "-text=Hello from Kyma!"
        ports:
        - containerPort: 5678
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: hello-kyma-service
  namespace: demo-app
spec:
  selector:
    app: hello-kyma
  ports:
  - protocol: TCP
    port: 80
    targetPort: 5678
---
apiVersion: gateway.kyma-project.io/v2alpha1
kind: APIRule
metadata:
  name: hello-kyma-api
  namespace: demo-app
spec:
  gateway: kyma-system/kyma-gateway
  hosts:
    - hello-kyma.<YOUR_CLUSTER_DOMAIN>
  service:
    name: hello-kyma-service
    port: 80
  rules:
    - path: /*
      methods: ["GET"]
      noAuth: true

Deploy it:

kubectl apply -f deployment.yaml

# Wait for pod with Istio sidecar
kubectl get pods -n demo-app
# You should see 2/2 containers (app + istio-proxy)

# Test your app
curl https://hello-kyma.<YOUR_CLUSTER_DOMAIN>

Key differences:

  • CF Router → Istio + APIRule
  • CF routes → APIRule hosts
  • Automatic SSL in both, but APIRule uses noAuth: true vs App Router patterns

Pattern 2: Service Bindings - From VCAP_SERVICES to Kubernetes Secrets

CF Pattern:

cf create-service xsuaa application myxsuaa
cf bind-service myapp myxsuaa
# Credentials appear in VCAP_SERVICES environment variable

Kyma Pattern: In Kyma, service bindings create Kubernetes Secrets.

apiVersion: services.cloud.sap.com/v1
kind: ServiceInstance
metadata:
  name: kyma-xsuaa
  namespace: demo-app
spec:
  serviceOfferingName: xsuaa
  servicePlanName: application
  parameters:
    xsappname: kyma-demo-xsuaa
    tenant-mode: dedicated
    scopes:
      - name: "$XSAPPNAME.Read"
        description: "Read permission"
    role-templates:
      - name: Reader
        scope-references:
          - "$XSAPPNAME.Read"
---
apiVersion: services.cloud.sap.com/v1
kind: ServiceBinding
metadata:
  name: kyma-xsuaa-binding
  namespace: demo-app
spec:
  serviceInstanceName: kyma-xsuaa
  secretName: kyma-xsuaa-secret

Deploy it:

kubectl apply -f xsuaa-instance.yaml

# Wait for ready (takes 1-2 minutes)
kubectl get serviceinstance kyma-xsuaa -n demo-app -w

Now use the credentials in your app - you have two options:

Option 1: Environment Variables

env:
- name: XSUAA_CLIENTID
  valueFrom:
    secretKeyRef:
      name: kyma-xsuaa-secret
      key: clientid
- name: XSUAA_CLIENTSECRET
  valueFrom:
    secretKeyRef:
      name: kyma-xsuaa-secret
      key: clientsecret

Option 2: Volume Mounts

volumeMounts:
- name: xsuaa-volume
  mountPath: /etc/secrets/xsuaa
  readOnly: true
volumes:
- name: xsuaa-volume
  secret:
    secretName: kyma-xsuaa-secret

Then read credentials from files:

const fs = require('fs');
const clientId = fs.readFileSync('/etc/secrets/xsuaa/clientid', 'utf8');
const clientSecret = fs.readFileSync('/etc/secrets/xsuaa/clientsecret', 'utf8');

Key differences:

Cloud Foundry Kyma

cf bind-serviceServiceBinding resource
VCAP_SERVICES JSONKubernetes Secret
Auto-injected as env varMount as volume OR env vars
App parses JSONApp reads individual keys

Pattern 3: Pre-Runtime Configuration - From .profile to Init Containers

CF Pattern: Create a .profile script in your app directory:

#!/bin/bash
echo "Decrypting secrets..."
# Runs before app starts, in same container

Kyma Pattern: Use Kubernetes Init Containers that run before your main app:

apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: init-demo
  namespace: demo-app
spec:
  template:
    spec:
      # Init container runs FIRST
      initContainers:
      - name: decrypt-config
        image: busybox:latest
        command:
        - sh
        - -c
        - |
          echo "Init container running..."
          # Decrypt secrets, download certs, etc.
          cat /encrypted/config.txt | base64 -d > /decrypted/config.txt
          echo "Runtime info: $(date)" >> /decrypted/runtime-info.txt
          echo "Init complete!"
        volumeMounts:
        - name: encrypted-volume
          mountPath: /encrypted
        - name: decrypted-volume
          mountPath: /decrypted
      
      # Main app runs AFTER init completes
      containers:
      - name: app
        image: myapp:latest
        volumeMounts:
        - name: decrypted-volume
          mountPath: /decrypted
          readOnly: true
      
      volumes:
      - name: encrypted-volume
        configMap:
          name: encrypted-config
      - name: decrypted-volume
        emptyDir: {}

Key differences:

CF .profile Kyma Init Containers

Shell script onlyAny container image
Same containerSeparate container
Sequential onlyMultiple init containers (chained)
No resource limitsCPU/memory limits per init

Real-world use cases:

  • Decrypt Credential Store secrets
  • Download certificates from external CA
  • Run database migrations
  • Generate dynamic configuration
  • Wait for dependencies to be ready

Pattern 4: Credential Store Integration

In CF, you might fetch credentials in your .profile script. In Kyma, use an init container to fetch from Credential Store and write to a shared volume.

First, create the Credential Store service:

apiVersion: services.cloud.sap.com/v1
kind: ServiceInstance
metadata:
  name: credstore
  namespace: demo-app
spec:
  serviceOfferingName: credstore
  servicePlanName: trial
---
apiVersion: services.cloud.sap.com/v1
kind: ServiceBinding
metadata:
  name: credstore-binding
  namespace: demo-app
spec:
  serviceInstanceName: credstore
  secretName: credstore-secret

Then fetch credentials in an init container:

spec:
  initContainers:
  - name: fetch-credentials
    image: curlimages/curl:latest
    command:
    - sh
    - -c
    - |
      # Get OAuth token
      OAUTH_URL="$(cat /credstore/url | sed 's|/api.*||')/oauth/token"
      TOKEN=$(curl -s -X POST "$OAUTH_URL" \
        -d "grant_type=client_credentials" \
        -d "client_id=$(cat /credstore/username)" \
        -d "client_secret=$(cat /credstore/password)" \
        | jq -r '.access_token')
      
      # Fetch credential
      CRED_URL=$(cat /credstore/url)
      curl -s "$CRED_URL/password?name=database-password" \
        -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
        | jq -r '.value' > /secrets/db-password
    volumeMounts:
    - name: credstore-creds
      mountPath: /credstore
      readOnly: true
    - name: runtime-secrets
      mountPath: /secrets
  
  containers:
  - name: app
    image: myapp:latest
    volumeMounts:
    - name: runtime-secrets
      mountPath: /secrets
      readOnly: true
  
  volumes:
  - name: credstore-creds
    secret:
      secretName: credstore-secret
  - name: runtime-secrets
    emptyDir: {}

Why this matters:

  • Credentials NOT stored in Kubernetes Secrets (more secure)
  • Credentials fetched at runtime (can rotate without redeploying)
  • Main app doesn't need Credential Store SDK
  • Init container separates credential management from app logic

Pattern 5: Destination Service Integration

CF Pattern: In CF, you use the App Router with @sap/approuter package, which handles Destination Service integration automatically.

Kyma Pattern: In Kyma, you need to call the Destination Service API directly from your application.

First, create the destination in BTP Cockpit:

  1. Go to Connectivity → Destinations
  2. Create destination:
    • Name: backend-api
    • URL: https://api.example.com
    • Authentication: NoAuthentication
    • Additional Property: forwardAuthToken = true

Then create the Destination service instance:

apiVersion: services.cloud.sap.com/v1
kind: ServiceInstance
metadata:
  name: destination
  namespace: demo-app
spec:
  serviceOfferingName: destination
  servicePlanName: lite
---
apiVersion: services.cloud.sap.com/v1
kind: ServiceBinding
metadata:
  name: destination-binding
  namespace: demo-app
spec:
  serviceInstanceName: destination
  secretName: destination-secret

Use it in your Node.js app:

const express = require('express');
const axios = require('axios');
const fs = require('fs');
const app = express();

// Read Destination Service credentials from mounted secret
const destCreds = {
  uri: fs.readFileSync('/etc/secrets/destination/uri', 'utf8').trim(),
  clientid: fs.readFileSync('/etc/secrets/destination/clientid', 'utf8').trim(),
  clientsecret: fs.readFileSync('/etc/secrets/destination/clientsecret', 'utf8').trim(),
  url: fs.readFileSync('/etc/secrets/destination/url', 'utf8').trim()
};

app.get('/call-backend', async (req, res) => {
  try {
    // 1. Get OAuth token for Destination Service
    const tokenResponse = await axios.post(
      `${destCreds.url}/oauth/token`,
      new URLSearchParams({
        grant_type: 'client_credentials',
        client_id: destCreds.clientid,
        client_secret: destCreds.clientsecret
      }),
      { headers: { 'Content-Type': 'application/x-www-form-urlencoded' } }
    );
    
    const token = tokenResponse.data.access_token;
    
    // 2. Get destination configuration
    const destResponse = await axios.get(
      `${destCreds.uri}/destination-configuration/v1/destinations/backend-api`,
      { headers: { Authorization: `Bearer ${token}` } }
    );
    
    const backendUrl = destResponse.data.destinationConfiguration.URL;
    
    // 3. Call backend via destination
    const backendResponse = await axios.get(`${backendUrl}/posts/1`);
    
    res.json({
      message: 'Success!',
      data: backendResponse.data
    });
    
  } catch (error) {
    res.status(500).json({ error: error.message });
  }
});

app.listen(8080);

Deploy with the secret mounted:

apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: destination-demo
spec:
  template:
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: app
        image: node:18-alpine
        volumeMounts:
        - name: destination-creds
          mountPath: /etc/secrets/destination
          readOnly: true
      volumes:
      - name: destination-creds
        secret:
          secretName: destination-secret
---
apiVersion: gateway.kyma-project.io/v2alpha1
kind: APIRule
metadata:
  name: destination-demo-api
spec:
  gateway: kyma-system/kyma-gateway
  hosts:
    - destination-demo.<YOUR_CLUSTER_DOMAIN>
  service:
    name: destination-demo-service
    port: 80
  rules:
    - path: /*
      methods: ["GET"]
      noAuth: true

Key differences:

Cloud Foundry Kyma

App Router handles itManual API calls
@sap/approuter packageCustom integration code
Auto-configured routesExplicit OAuth + API calls

Summary: CF vs Kyma Migration Checklist

Feature Cloud Foundry Kyma Equivalent

Deploycf pushkubectl apply -f deployment.yaml
Service Bindingcf bind-serviceServiceBinding resource
CredentialsVCAP_SERVICES JSON env varKubernetes Secret (volume/env)
Pre-runtime.profile scriptInit Containers
RoutingCF Router + routesIstio + APIRule
AuthenticationApp RouterAPIRule accessStrategies
Namespace isolationCF Spaces (shared network)K8s Namespaces (network policies)
Scalingcf scalekubectl scale or HPA

Key Takeaways

  1. Istio is mandatory: Always enable istio-injection=enabled on namespaces before deploying apps that need external access.

  2. Secrets are files, not JSON: In Kyma, service credentials are individual files in a mounted volume, not a single JSON object.

  3. Init containers are powerful: They're not just a .profile replacement—they can use any container image, have independent resource limits, and can be chained.

  4. Manual integration required: Unlike CF's App Router, Kyma requires you to integrate with BTP services (like Destination Service) directly via API calls.

  5. Kubernetes-native: Kyma uses standard Kubernetes patterns. If you know Kubernetes, you know Kyma. If you don't, learning Kyma teaches you portable Kubernetes skills.

Conclusion

The patterns in this blog give you a foundation to migrate existing CF apps to Kyma. Start with simple apps, master these five patterns, then tackle more complex workloads.

entry.title: Australia Localization for S/4 HANA Private Cloud 2023 FPS02 - AU Tax Procedure Scope Items missing
entry.content:
We wish to activate Localisation Best Practices for Taxation on Sales/Purchase as well Compliance reporting for Australia on a Global instance of S/4 HANA Private Cloud 2023.

In previous versions, I believe this functionality such as preconfigured Tax Codes (e.g 10% GST on Sales) came with the activation of  Scope items for Local Best Practices. The Tax procedure is there in the IMG (TAXAU) however the tax codes are missing. I understand that with the current version of SAP, the new tax enhanced procedure for Australia is OTXAU (per OSS Note 3577582). This is missing from our client configuration. 

We can configure Tax Codes in TAXAU, but it would preferable to activate a best practise as the config needs to be aligned to the compliance reports (BAS Statement etc). OSS 3393679 provides the definitions that we can use for configuration, but it's unclear if it is better under TAXAU or the new procedure OTXAU.

Is there a localisation scope item or note that I can provide to our global Basis Team for installing the Australian Tax procedures fully?

Similarly, I understand that S/4 2023 has Simplified Implementation of Framework features for Compliance Reporting 

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_ON-PREMISE/3b2c091e13434f9ab2fb53d147ba5e9f/1d97de32272248cd92accc4dfc0bfcd9.html?version=2023.latest) . In order to install the following relevant AU reports 

  • PTR
  • TPAR
  • PAYG Withholding
  • BAS

Currently, when I review the configuration there is no Reporting Entity AU_RPG_ENT, so it appears this localisation content is not activated.

In S/4 2023, will using the Framework features to activate the reports provide all the relevant configuration?

entry.title: Issue with Goal linking feature in SAP SF
entry.content:
Hello Experts,

We have a scenario where the goal linking should happen between employee and manager and not with anyone else in the organization. The permissions for employee's goal plan is given in such a way that Manager, Talent admin and HRBP can view employees goal plan. Employees can view manager's goal plan. 

We tried restricting cascade-align permission through XML:

<permission for="cascade-align">
<description><![CDATA[Anyone may cascade a goal to anyone.]]></description>
 <role-name><![CDATA[EM]]></role-name>
<role-name><![CDATA[E]]></role-name>
</permission>

With the above XML code the employee is not able to Link goals with Managers.

Employees were able to link goals to their Manager's goal plan only when Cascade-align permission is granted to everyone. So this leads to Talent admins and others who have access to employees goal plan to link goals.

Any suggestion on how this can be achieved?

entry.title: I Asked 50 SAP Professionals What Scares Them About AI. Here's What They Said.
entry.content:
How This All Started

Picture this. I am sitting in a room full of SAP consultants, project managers, functional leads, and business analysts at a community meetup last quarter. Someone casually drops the words “SAP AI” into the conversation and the entire energy in the room shifts.

Not with excitement. With something closer to anxiety.

That moment stayed with me. So I did something about it. Over the next few weeks I reached out to 50 SAP professionals across industries, experience levels, and geographies. I asked them one simple question:

What honestly scares you about AI in SAP?

No filters. No corporate answers. Just real people telling me real things.

What came back was fascinating, uncomfortable, validating, and hopeful all at once. Let me share it with you exactly as I heard it.

Before We Get Into It, A Quick Question For You

Poll: What is your gut feeling when you hear “SAP AI” right now?

  • Honestly a little scared
  • Curious but uncertain
  • Indifferent, still watching
  • Genuinely excited

Pick one. We will revisit your answer at the end. You might surprise yourself.

Fear #1: Am I Going to Become Irrelevant?

This came up more than anything else. 31 out of 50 professionals mentioned job security in some form. Not always directly. Sometimes it sounded like this:

“I have spent 14 years mastering SAP FI. If AI can do what I do in seconds, what exactly am I bringing to the table?”

Or this:

“My client literally asked me last month if they still needed my team once Joule was fully live. That question kept me up at night.”

If you felt this way and said it out loud, that took courage. The fear is not irrational. But history shows that every major enterprise technology shift created more specialized roles than it eliminated. SAP itself was once feared as a job killer. Today it supports millions of professionals globally.

AI will not replace the SAP professional who understands the business deeply. It will replace the SAP professional who refuses to evolve.

The question is not whether AI will change your role. It already is. The question is whether you will shape that change or be shaped by it.

A Moment to Reflect

Poll: How worried are you personally about AI affecting your SAP career?

  • Very worried
  • A little worried
  • Not worried, I see the opportunity
  • Not sure yet

Whatever you picked is valid. You are not alone.

Fear #2: What Happens When AI Gets It Wrong?

This concern came mostly from technical professionals. 24 out of 50 raised accuracy and accountability risks. One senior SAP architect said:

“My worry is not that AI will be wrong. My worry is that it will be wrong confidently. And nobody will check it because everyone assumes the machine is right.”

We have all seen what happens when a financial posting goes wrong in SAP. Month end chaos. Audit questions. Stressed finance teams. Now imagine that at AI speed and scale.

This is a real concern. The answer is not to avoid AI. The answer is governance inside your SAP landscape. Human checkpoints. Confidence thresholds. Exception workflows. These are foundational controls.

Fear #3: Our Data Is a Mess and Nobody Wants to Say It

22 out of 50 professionals mentioned data quality as their hidden fear. One project manager said:

“Everyone in leadership is excited about SAP AI. Nobody wants to be the person who says our master data is a disaster.”

AI amplifies whatever already exists in your SAP system. Clean data gets smarter. Messy data gets messier faster. Years of inconsistent master records cannot be covered by an AI layer.

Before AI deployment, do the foundational work. Run data quality audits. Fix master data governance. Strengthen controls. It is not glamorous, but it works.

Quick Check In

Poll: How would you describe your SAP data quality today?

  • Clean and governed
  • Mixed quality
  • Messy
  • Not sure

Fear #4: Change Management Will Kill This Before It Starts

19 out of 50 professionals focused on people risk, not technology risk. One change consultant shared:

“I have seen strong SAP programs fail because users never trusted the system. AI is even more personal. If users feel replaced, adoption drops quietly.”

Resistance is often silent. Users override suggestions. Avoid tools. Work around the system. Adoption drops and leadership asks why.

If you are leading SAP AI initiatives, your first meeting should be with end users, not vendors.

Fear #5: Leadership Thinks AI Is a Magic Button

17 out of 50 professionals expressed leadership expectation gaps. One said:

“Our CEO declared we would be AI first this year. We are not even fully on S/4HANA yet.”

There is a gap between boardroom AI excitement and SAP reality. Urgency without readiness creates burnout. Realism without excitement creates inertia. Strong programs balance both.

Implementers must educate upward with clarity and courage.

The Other Side of Fear

After asking about fears, I asked what gives them hope. The tone shifted:

“If AI removes manual reconciliations, I can focus on analysis.”

“Junior consultants adapt quickly. That gives me confidence.”

“I resisted Fiori at first too. Now I cannot imagine working without it.”

Every SAP disruption felt uncomfortable before it felt normal. This one is no different.

entry.title: Transport Developer Extensibility Artifacts in SAP Public Cloud
entry.content:
Transport Developer Extensibility Artifacts

 Introduction: 

In the SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud 3-System Landscape (3SL), developers often face challenges when transporting extensibility artifacts across environments. While the customizing tenant (client 100) is dedicated to configuration, the development tenant (client 080) is where ABAP coding and developer extensibility take place.

This blog aims to provide a high-level overview of how developer extensibility artifacts (such as custom CDS views and service bindings) can be moved upstream in the landscape.

It also highlights important restrictions, such as the absence of a transport path from ADT-created objects into the customizing tenant, and points to SAP Note 3324929 for further clarification. By walking through a practical example, we will explore the steps required to expose CDS views securely via OData services and ensure smooth integration with external systems.

To set the context, here’s a quick snapshot of the artifacts available through developer extensibility and key user extensibility.

Detailed Explanation:

When working with developer extensibility in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud, one common requirement is to expose custom CDS views so they can be consumed by integration teams. However, due to technical restrictions in the cloud environment, you cannot directly publish an OData service from a CDS view using the @odata.publish: true annotation. Instead, the recommended approach is to expose the CDS view through a service binding.

Let’s walk through the process step by step. We begin in the development tenant (client 080).

  • Create a Custom CDS view in ADT
  • Create Service Definition to expose your CDS view
  • Create Service Binding – Select Binding type as per your need. In this example, OData V4 - Web API is selected
  • Publish your Service Binding
  • It will then generate an Inbound service
  • Security is a critical aspect here. To ensure that your OData service is protected, you must configure authentication, which forms the basis for secure external consumption.
  • To do this you need to create a Communication Scenario in Development tenant (Client 080)
  • In ADT, create a Communication Scenario.
  • Give this new Communication Scenario a name starting with "Z". e.g., ZSAP_COM_825
  • Add needed Inbound Service ID:
    1. Click the Inbound configuration
    2. Click the Add button
    3. Click the Browse button
    4. Select the S_PRIVILEGED_SQL1

       

  • Add Authorization Object:
    1. Click the Authorization configuration
    2. Click the Insert button
    3. Search for SQL_VIEW
    4. Select SQL_VIEW

       

    5. Expand Authorization 0001
      1. SQL_SCHEMA: The name of the Service Binding (exposed as a schema) that you want to grant access to

         

      2. SQL_VIEW: Comma-separated list of all CDS view entities to which you want to grant access or * (if you want to allow access to all CDS view entities in the service definition)

         

      3. SQL_VIEWOP: Choose at least one option

                               For Remote Tables → Choose SELECT

                             

                             For Replication Flows → Choose REPLICATE

                            

  • Click the Publish Locally button

You are all done from Development tenant (Client 080). The next stage takes place in the customizing tenant (client 100).

Login to your Customizing tenant.

  • Create the Communication arrangement using the scenario delivered from 080
  • Open the Communication Arrangements APP.
  • Click the New button.
  • Search for the required scenario.
  • Click the required scenario.
  • Rename the Arrangement Name.
  • Click the Create button.
     

     

  • Configurate the Communication Arrangement
  1. Select the Customer Communication system.
  2. Record the API-URL. e.g.,  https://myXXXXXX-api.s4hana.cloud.sap

     

    Click the Save button.
     

Congratulations 🎉🎉, you are all set now and ready to push your transport into upward environments. Now you can get CDS data from this API. You can test it from Postman and connect to this API using IS tools for example CPI.

Tip💡
If your Communication Scenario does not appear in client 100, then most likely you have not published it in Development tenant (client 080).

 

Additional information:

Why You can't See Communication Arrangements in the Export Software Collection App

SAP clearly states that:

  • The Export Software Collection app is used only for key‑user extensibility objects (custom CDS, custom fields, custom logic, custom business objects, UI changes, etc.)
  • Communication Arrangements are not part of key user extensibility; therefore, they don’t appear in software collections.

This is explained in the SAP 3‑System Landscape transport overview.

 

Object Type

Can appear in Software Collection?

Transport Method

Custom CDS (Key User)

Yes

Software Collection

Custom Business Objects

Yes

Software Collection

UI Flex Changes

Yes

Software Collection

Custom Communication Scenarios (ADT)

No

Developer Transport

SQL Service Bindings (ADT)

No

Developer Transport

For this simple reason:

👉 Communication Arrangements do not appear in Software Collections
👉 Communication Arrangements do not move inside developer transports
👉 Communication Arrangements must be created manually in every system

SAP’s official help states Communication Arrangements must be configured through the Communication Arrangements app, per system.

Clean and Simple summary:

Object

Transportable

Moves Automatically?

Appears in TEST?

Action needed

Custom Communication Scenario (ADT)

Yes (developer transport)

Yes

Visible

Nothing

Communication Arrangement (client 100)

No

No

Not Visible

Should be created in each client

 

So, What Must You Do Now? (Correct Next Step)

In the TEST system, you must now manually recreate the Communication Arrangement:

  1. Log in to TEST system
  2. Open Communication Arrangements app
  3. Click New
  4. Select your transported scenario
  5. Create the arrangement
  6. Re-select:
  7. Communication System
  8. Communication User
  9. Authentication method
  10. Any inbound/outbound parameters
  11. Save

 

Conclusion:

Transporting developer extensibility artifacts in SAP Public Cloud requires a clear understanding of what can and cannot be moved automatically. While custom CDS views, service definitions, and communication scenarios created in ADT are transportable through developer transports, communication arrangements remain system-specific and must be recreated manually in each environment. This distinction is crucial for ensuring secure and consistent integration across the landscape.

By following the outlined steps: creating CDS views, service bindings, and communication scenarios in the development tenant, then configuring communication arrangements in the customizing tenant, you can successfully expose your CDS data via OData services and make it consumable for integration tools like SAP CPI.

Ultimately, this process reinforces the importance of separating extensibility artifacts from environment-specific configurations, ensuring both flexibility and compliance in SAP’s cloud transport model.

Hello @SFerrand

That API helps but it does not replace the full migration approach for your case. You linked CE_FIXEDASSET_0001 CreateFixedAsset. That API is to create/change fixed asset master data scenarios.

See the following references for more details: https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3219541

https://api.sap.com/search?filter=(Products%20eq%20(SAP%20S%2F4HANA%20Cloud))&searchterm=fixed%20asset&tab=api

As suggested, use the Fixed Asset Migration objects in Migration Cockpit and split the load into batches you can control.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Re: Material Document button not visible in QA32 if the G...
entry.content:
Why the Material Document Button Disappears

  • GR against HU (PRDI, one HU at a time):
    • The inspection lot is created at HU level.
    • However, the material document link is not directly associated with the inspection lot in QA32 because the posting logic in EWM does not update the QM lot with the material document reference in this scenario.
    • Essentially, the HU-level GR triggers the inspection lot but does not pass the material document number into the lot header.
  • GR against the whole delivery (Goods Receipt + Save):
  • The system posts a consolidated material document for the delivery.
  • This document is correctly linked to the inspection lot, so QA32 shows the “Material Document” button.

Resolution Options

To make the material document visible in QA32 even when posting GR against HUs:

  1. Check Customizing in QM-EWM Integration:
    • In transaction SPRO, under Quality Management → Quality Inspection → Inspection Lot Creation → Goods Receipt, ensure that the settings for HU-based GR are aligned to update the material document reference.
    • Some implementations require an enhancement (user exit/BAdI) to populate the material document number when inspection lots are created at HU level.
  2. Use BAdI QEVA0007 or QPL1

entry.title: Re: Deep Dive into SAP Datasphere Object Store of BDC: Benefits, Architecture and implementation
entry.content:
@mdelibasic

there are missing considerations: whenever you write something into a local table file via replication flow, you need the merge task, the merge task rewrite the entire table into another file/files and keep the old data for at least 7 days.

So 1TB of object store is consumed by the older version of the data, if you load the data once a day, you effectively have 1TB / 7 = 142,8GB. => 0.525 CU / GB.

Unfortunately the storage component is the smallest component in terms of costs, we found that for a table with 150M rows (1.5GB), the costs of only keep updated the data in the object store, extracting it from the source system is 6x higher than keeping it in memory (without transformation successive data read (SQL on file)). the big part of the cost is from Compute and in some cases (fragmentation) from API call.
We tested partitions / delta loading, deferred merge, and changing apache spark application level, and many options (vaccuum / optimize), nothing reduce the costs effectively. 

it seems related to the size of the apache spark cluster that is instantiated at runtime (too big).

I really appreciate if other people try to measure capacity units consumption in "System Monitor -> Capacities" and found different results.

entry.title: Re: Delivery Locked on Auto Invoice via BAPI
entry.content:
The most stable solution is to decouple invoice creation from the immediate posting. Configure your output type to trigger invoice creation after commit (via update task or scheduled output processing). This way:

  • Delivery posting completes successfully.

  • Lock is released.

  • Invoice creation runs without collision.

  • Document flow updates correctly.

entry.title: Re: Unable to add New EWM warehouse number to New Busines...
entry.content:
Dear @kkumara,

In Public Cloud embedded EWM the Default Party Entitled to Dispose (PED) in CBC ID 102657 Set Warehouse Address is not an arbitrary Business Partner. The system only allows the BP that represents the plant maintained as Customer No. Plant for the plant(s) assigned to the warehouse. If you enter a regular new BP even a customer/supplier it is rejected with error so maintain Customer No. Plant for the relevant plant in ID 101926 Define Shipping Data for Plants then return to CBC ID 102657 Set Warehouse Address and select that plant-representing BP as Dflt Pty Entld

More details in SAP Note below:

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3622534

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Re: SAP BTP ABAP Environment – New software delivery option “Add-On Product”
entry.content:
Hello @Atul_Joshi85 ,

1. The partner has complete freedom of choice, and there is no dependency on SAP BTP ABAP environment releases. In contrast to the classic ABAP approach in on-premise where the add-on is tightly coupled to the underlying ABAP platform release, that's different with ABAP Cloud approach and the SAP BTP ABAP Environment: ABAP Cloud enforces the usage of released API in partner add-ons and SAP ensures that these released APIs remain stable. 

2. Only the customer chooses when to import the new version into their system. Therefore, the partner may need to offer and maintain multiple versions. This also means that the partner is responsible for defining the support period. We do not plan a feature that the partner is allowed to push a new version into the customer systems. The deployment is always initiated by the customer.

entry.title: ABAP in Eclipse logon requesting credentials repeatedly
entry.content:
Hi SAP Community,

I am experiencing a persistent login loop when trying to use ABAP Development Tools (ADT) in Eclipse.

The Issue:

When Eclipse triggers the logon prompt, it redirects me to the browser. However, the logon page refuses to sign me in. When I enter my username and password, the page simply refreshes without logging me in.

What I have observed:

  • If I try to log in using my username, I receive the error:
    "We couldn't authenticate you."

  • However, if I log in using my email address with the same password, it works successfully.

  • This setup was working perfectly until today. Previously, I was not prompted for browser login at all.

Steps already taken:

  1. Cleared browser cache and cookies.

  2. Reset SAP password.

  3. Tried different browsers.

Despite these steps, the issue persists in Eclipse. It seems like the ADT logon window is rejecting the credentials or failing the authentication handshake.

Has anyone encountered this recently in ADT?

Any guidance would be greatly appreciated.

entry.title: 🇭🇺 Build AI services using CAP and Generative AI Hub (Budapest, Hungary)
entry.content:
This SAP CodeJam event is on the topic of Build AI services using the SAP Cloud Application Programming Model, SAP AI Core and Generative AI Hub. It is beneficial if you have some basic knowledge about JavaScript as we're using the SAP Cloud SDK for AI with the JavaScript variant.

This CodeJam introduces attendees to aspects of exposing generative artificial intelligence (genAI) capabilities to execute tasks using a given business context.

During this event, you'll become acquainted with the theory behind genAI, SAP AI Core, SAP AI Launchpad, and learn how to use the SAP Cloud Application Programming Model to expose AI capabilities through your own OData service.

While working through the exercises, you'll become comfortable creating CDS-based service definitions and integrating them into the SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP) and SAP AI Core Foundation Models.

This is an in-person event only (not virtual) and is planned for Wednesday April 29th, 2026 in Budapest, Hungary 🇭🇺 from 10:00 am - 04:00 pm. The workshop site is the House of Business Bank Center in Budapest. The event is kindly being hosted by Nagarro.

The event will be held in english.

The instructor is the Developer Advocate @KRiedelsheimer .

Please note that seating is limited - we can only accomodate 25 attendees. Register here in the Community Event using the RSVP functionality.

entry.title: Using Client Certificate Authentication in SAP CPI HTTP Receiver Adapter
entry.content:
Introduction

Hi friends, in this blog lets see how to configure the HTTP receiver adapter using client certificate auth type and step by step explain how a PKCS#12 (.p12) certificate is stored in the Keystore, which I have explored in my recent Integration flow development.

What is Client Certificate Authentication?

Client certificate authentication, also known as a mutual authentication method / mutual TLS (mTLS), ensures that:

  • The client (SAP CPI) authenticates itself to the server

  • The server also presents its certificate to CPI

  • Both sides trust each other using certificates issued by trusted CAs

Unlike basic or OAuth authentication, client certificate authentication relies on X.509 certificates and private keys, not usernames or tokens.
For more information about client certificate authentication, refer to the link:

https://help.sap.com/docs/integration-suite/sap-integration-suite/client-certificate-authentication-outbound?locale=en-US&state=PRODUCTION&version=CLOUD 

Files Provided by the Partner

Our 3rd party client (receiver system) has provided 

  • certificate.crt — signed client certificate (after providing the signing request)

  • private_key.key — private key (We need to generate this via OpenSSL if it is a Production tenant)

  • certificate-chain.pem — intermediate/root CA certificates

To establish the connection, all three must be updated in the tenant keystore, since SAP CPI does not accept .key files directly, these files must be converted directly into a PKCS#12 (.p12) format.

Creating the P12 File

The .p12 file contains:

  • Client certificate (eg, certificate.crt)

  • Private key (eg, private_key.key)

  • CA certificate chain (Intermediate + Root certificates) (eg, certificate-chain.pem) 

Using OpenSSL /Command prompt:

Open the command prompt from the same folder in which all these files are stored. 

Enter the command below, and it will ask you to set the password. After you enter the password, it generates the p12 file in the same folder.

openssl pkcs12 -export \
-in certificate.crt \
-inkey private_key.key \
-certfile certificate-chain.pem \
-out client.p12 \
-name "client-cert"


Note: An export password is required and will be used later during upload into CPI.

Uploading the Certificate to SAP CPI Keystore

  1. Log in to SAP Cloud Integration

  2. Go to Monitor → Keystore

  3. Choose Add → Key Pair

  4. Upload client.p12

  5. Enter the export password

  6. Save

The certificate is now securely stored and managed by CPI.

 

Configuring the HTTP Receiver Adapter

In your integration flow:

  1. Add an HTTP Receiver Adapter

  2. Set Authentication to: Client Certificate

  3. In the Credential Name field, select the alias of the uploaded .p12 from the Keystore.

 

Security Best Practices

  • Protect the .p12 password

  • Use separate certificates for PROD and NON-PROD

  • Monitor certificate expiry dates

Conclusion

Client certificate authentication is a secure and reliable option. Using a P12 certificate from the keystore with the HTTP Receiver Adapter keeps authentication simple and secure.

Thank you for reading this blog. I hope this helps you with your integration. When I started this integration development, I uploaded all the keystore documents separately, but it still ended up failing. Also, I couldn’t find any reference blog on how to handle this. So, I want to share this with others. If you feel this is a long procedure (as I do), please share your ideas and feedback in the comments.

entry.title: Google Pub/Sub Adapter in SAP Integration Suite:
entry.content:
Google Pub/Sub Adapter in SAP Integration Suite:

 Google Pub/Sub is a fully managed messaging service that enables asynchronous communication between applications. With SAP Integration Suite, SAP provides a native Google Pub/Sub Adapter that allows seamless publishing and subscribing to events.

What is Google Pub/Sub?

  • As a Publishers we can send messages to a Topic
  • As a Subscribers we can consume/receive messages from a Topic Subscription

We can Also handle Acknowledgement with in google pub/Sub adapter which will be like Acknowledge after Successful Processing, Acknowledge Immediately, and Do not Acknowledge. 

  • Acknowledge after Successful Processing:
    • The message is only acknowledged to Google Pub/Sub after it has been fully and successfully processed by the SAP Integration Suite iFlow.
    • If processing fails, the message will not be acknowledged and will be redelivered by Google Pub/Sub.
  • Acknowledge Immediately:
    • The message is acknowledged as soon as it is received by the adapter, regardless of the subsequent processing outcome in the iFlow.
    • This means the message will not be redelivered even if the integration flow fails later.
  • Do not Acknowledge:
    • The adapter does not send an acknowledgment.
    • This is typically used in specific scenarios where manual acknowledgment or another mechanism is handled externally.

In my case I am creating a sample scenario where I am sending the IDOC Item data to the external service which is a third party application with micro services

  1. At the sender side we are using the IDOC Handler (from a common Iflow) which then routes the data to Process Direct Adapter to pull the Idoc  data to our Iflow.
  2. In the second step we are using the content modifier Set SAP Message Headers and setting the Header Parameters

  1. As we have few fields to be mapped to the target fields, we need to add a Message mapping step and map the IDOC fields to the target structure
  2. Convert the mapped xml to the equivalent JSON as the target application is accepting only JSON format
  3. Within the JSON data we need to add an array which is not being created by default. For which you will have to write a groovy script and add the array

import com.sap.it.script.v2.api.Message

import groovy.json.JsonSlurper

import groovy.json.JsonOutput

 

def Message processData(Message message) {

    def body = message.getBody(java.lang.String)

 

    def jsonSlurper = new JsonSlurper()

    def input = jsonSlurper.parseText(body)

 

    // 1. Process ItemPackage: Remove 'element' and make it an array

    if (input.Data?.ItemPackage?.element) {

        // Extract the content of 'element'

        def elementContent = input.Data.ItemPackage.element

        // Reassign ItemPackage as an array containing that content

        input.Data.ItemPackage = [elementContent]

    } else if (input.Data?.ItemPackage) {

        // If ItemPackage exists but 'element' doesn't, just ensure it's an array

        input.Data.ItemPackage = input.Data.ItemPackage instanceof List ?

                                 input.Data.ItemPackage : [input.Data.ItemPackage]

    }

 

    // 2. Process Data: Convert the single occurence of Data into an array

    if (input.Data != null && !(input.Data instanceof List)) {

        input.Data = [input.Data]

    }

 

    // 3. Serialize back to JSON

    message.setBody(JsonOutput.prettyPrint(JsonOutput.toJson(input)))

   

    return message

}

 

  1. Create  a Receiver and connect the Google pub/Sub adapter with the below parameters

In processing tab you need to select the parameters:

 

Parameter 

Description 

Entity  

Select the entity for message processing:  

·        Topics 

·        Subscriptions  

Operation  

Select the operation to be performed.  

·        Publish Message

·        List  

Project ID  

Specify the Google Pub/Sub project ID.  

Topic ID  

Specify the Topic ID created on Google Pub/Sub service 

Operation Type  

Select the type of operation  

·        Basic 

·        Advanced  

Encode Data  

Select the checkbox to encode data to Base64 

Ordering key  

Specify the ordering key for this message.   

Message Attributes  

Specify multiple message attributes as key value pairs.

 Once the Configuration is done you should be able to trigger the IDOC and send the JSON message to the target application

Conclusion: 

  • The Google Pub/Sub adapter in SAP Cloud Integration (CI) enables real-time, event-driven communication between SAP systems and Google Cloud.
  • It allows CPI to seamlessly publish and consume messages at scale, supporting high-performance, responsive, and loosely coupled integrations.

entry.title: Commitments in Company Code
entry.content:
Good morning,

I am analyzing commitments when performing budget control for cost centers.

The other day, I had a question: when you create the purchase order, that is when the commitment is generated. That much is clear to me.

But what happens when you have the order approval in the workflow? I know that approval allows you to receive that order.

Let's imagine that it is rejected. We would no longer be able to receive the order, and from what I have seen, that commitment is not removed from the reports.

I also tried rejecting that order to see if it would be removed from the report, but it wasn't.

The only way I have found to remove it would be to mark the order position for deletion, in which case it would be removed.

I would like to know if there is a more automatic way to remove the commitment from the report once the order approval has been rejected.

Best regards.

Juan Antonio Moyano

entry.title: Re: Product Division in SAP S4 HANA Public Cloud
entry.content:
Below are couple of options

  • Create one order type for wholesale and one for retail. In wholesale order type, enforce “same division only”. In retail order type, allow mixed divisions. This is the cleanest way to differentiate because AUART is part of the VOV4 key.
  • Assign different item category groups to materials depending on whether they are sold in wholesale or retail. Maintain separate VOV4 entries for each item category group. This works if product sets are clearly segregated by channel, but adds master data maintenance.
  • If you want minimal master data maintenance, go with some custom logic where you can add a rule:
    • If distribution channel = wholesale → check all items belong to the same division, otherwise raise error.
    • If distribution channel = retail → skip the check.
    • This avoids multiplying order types or item category groups and directly enforces the business rule.

entry.title: Setting the right expectations around AI for code generation and getting past the inherent limits
entry.content:
With over two decades of working on AI, starting with my master's thesis on multi-layer perceptrons, I’m excited about the recent innovations in this area, especially as related to code generation. In this blog post, I want to share my perspective on using AI in a very specific context: coding with AI agents based on transformer models, as these are the primary tools we have at our disposal for this purpose today. I’ll start with why I use AI and then share some limitations of AI models and how to get around these pitfalls. Let’s start with a quick definition…

Generative AI is Only One Form of Artificial Intelligence

There's a lot of buzz around Artificial Intelligence (AI) these days, and it feels like every conversation about technology eventually lands on this topic. However, the term "AI" is often automatically conflated with "Generative AI" (GenAI). It's important to make a quick distinction. AI is the broad field of making machines smart. Machine Learning (ML) is a subset of AI that uses data to train models, and Deep Learning (DL) is a further subset of ML using neural networks. GenAI, which creates new content, is the latest evolution that has captured everyone's imagination.

Why I Use AI

AI is Practical

Large Language Models (LLMs) have provided us with an unprecedented natural language interface. This makes them incredibly powerful for tasks like web search and information retrieval, where a human can quickly verify the results. Coding is a domain where outputs are verifiable, which makes AI agents a significant efficiency booster. They aren't here to replace us, but to augment our abilities.

It's crucial to remember that LLMs have zero semantic understanding. They are masters of syntax, predicting the next word (or token) in a sequence through an auto-regressive process. They don't "understand" code; they predict what code should look like.

AI Adds Value to My Work

From my experience, AI brings tangible benefits to my development workflow. Here’s how:

  • Understanding Codebases: When jumping into a new or complex project, I use AI to get a quick summary of how different parts of the code interact.
  • Project Planning: Through agent orchestration, AI can help outline project structures, suggest potential components, and map out dependencies.
  • Code Documentation: This is a huge one. AI is exceptionally good at generating boilerplate documentation, docstrings, and comments, which frees me up to focus on the more complex descriptions.
  • Enforcing Guidelines: We can use AI to enforce Clean Core principles and other coding guidelines, ensuring that our code remains compliant and release-ready.

My History with AI

My journey with AI didn't start with the recent hype. It goes back to my college days, long before I entered the SAP ecosystem. My master's thesis was on multi-layer perceptrons and scaled conjugate gradient descent methods. Ever since then, I've been independently researching and following the advancements in this fascinating field.

Potential Pitfalls with AI and Take-aways

Differences Between Prompts and Target Language

Natural language is inherently ambiguous, imprecise, and often not concise. Science, and by extension software development, is about making formal, unambiguous statements. The greatest leaps in scientific history, like the formalization of algebra by Leibniz and Newton, came from moving away from rhetorical natural language and towards formal paradigms. The programming languages we use today are a natural evolution of this.

So-called "vibe coding," where one tries to build complex systems using only conversational language, is great for keynotes and simple examples but falls apart for enterprise-grade systems. I've seen attempts to "formalize" prompts to make agents more reliable, but this is a self-defeating exercise. You end up re-inventing a formal programming language, the very thing you were trying to avoid.

Take-away: This is a fundamental problem that won't be solved by simply scaling models. My practical advice is to iteratively update your agent's prompts and instructions as you code. Let the agent update its own instructions when it runs into issues. And most importantly: never commit code without verifying it yourself. Human experience is still an absolute necessity.

Predicting Syntax vs. Understanding Semantics

As mentioned, all modern coding agents are based on a transformer architecture. They predict syntax, token by token, without any semantic intelligence or understanding of programming metaconcepts. They operate on a vast joint probability distribution and can generalize well within that distribution.

However, when faced with an out-of-distribution (OOD) prompt, they "hallucinate." While syntax prediction without semantic understanding is often "good enough" to boost the efficiency of an experienced software engineer, it's irresponsible to leave critical design decisions to these agents without human oversight.

Take-away: Until a new paradigm emerges that gives AI true semantic understanding, human oversight is paramount. Used responsibly, AI is an incredibly rewarding tool that greatly supports our daily work.

Hallucinations (First and Higher Order)

OOD prompts lead to what I call "first-order hallucinations," where the LLM itself can't generalize and essentially makes things up. The answers are unreliable and require verification. Methods to minimize this include training on more data (scaling) or grounding the LLM with symbolic systems like databases or knowledge graphs.

This leads to what I term "higher-order hallucinations." The grounded data source might be perfectly accurate. However, to access it, the agent must translate your natural language prompt into a formal query. If it hallucinates during this translation, it can create an invalid query (getting no data) or a faulty query that retrieves the wrong correct data. The source is clean, but the retrieval is flawed.

Take-away: In the absence of a new paradigm, it's best to use simpler query sequences instead of one complex one. Simplify the toolset available to your agents and bundle related tasks into a single tool to minimize these translation errors.

Non-Determinism in Answers

While the forward-pass of a transformer is deterministic, the token decoding phase is not. This means if you prompt an LLM five times, you might get five different answers. For coding, this is problematic. Even with the temperature parameter set to zero, non-determinism persists.

Take-away: You never know if you've received the "best" possible generated code, and neither does the AI. This makes human verification absolutely critical, always. For mass refactoring, a robust review process is essential. Interestingly, review agents can help here, but the final decision must rest with a human.

Explainability & Verifiability

LLMs are notoriously black boxes. Billions of floating-point numbers don't offer a semantic explanation for why a particular answer was generated. Research into making these models more transparent is still in its infancy. The lack of semantic understanding, paired with non-determinism, makes true explainability incredibly difficult.

Take-away: For now, the only option for users is to wait for new research and paradigms to emerge.

TL;DR

  1. Human oversight is critical until AI gains a true semantic understanding, which it currently lacks.
  2. Use generative AI to speed your coding, but…
  3. Never commit code without verifying it yourself.
  4. Use simpler query sequences instead of one complex one.
  5. Have a robust code-review process with humans making the final decision.

Next Steps

It is irresponsible for any serious software engineer to ignore the current suite of AI tools, despite their shortcomings. Demonizing them is counter-productive. For those who know what they're doing, these tools are massive efficiency boosters. With responsible use and human oversight, they can be successfully integrated into enterprise workflows and offer many opportunities to improve the human-machine interface.

Outlook

We should not believe all the hype or inflate our expectations of what AI can realistically do today. At the same time, we shouldn't refrain from engaging with these tools in a meaningful way. We, the developers, are still the ones with the creativity, innovation, and semantic understanding. Let's use these tools to amplify that.

Happy coding!

What are your thoughts? Share your experiences with AI in the comments below!

entry.title: Re: Product Division in SAP S4 HANA Public Cloud
entry.content:
Dear @KrishnarajaBhat,

In Public Cloud one sales order belongs to exactly one Sales Area (Sales Org + Distribution Channel + Division). You can define common distribution channels / common divisions to reduce master data replication but the sales order is still created for a single sales area

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/a376cd9ea00d476b96f18dea1247e6a5/6f8bc95360267214e10000000a174cb4.html

IMPORTANT: SAP confirms that the division at header level and item level must be the same in a single sales order. The item division is derived from the product material master so if products are maintained with divisions 10/20/30/40 then mixing them in one sales order is technically not possible regardless of whether the order is Retail or Wholesale

SAP Note below:

https://me.sap.com/notes/0003443055

That is exactly why:

  • maintaining division in the product master enforces the no cross-division in one order behavior for everyone including Retail
  • If the item division derived from the material matches the sales order header division then the system will not restrict mixing based on division for both Wholesale and Retail
  • Common Division 00 does not solve mixing if items still carry different divisions

Option 1: Recommended and cleanest in Public Cloud

Don’t using Division as the product segregation attribute. Use one common division for the sales org so Retail can technically sell all products in one order and keep the 4 product families using a proper attribute such as:

  • Product Hierarchy (recommended) or
  • Material Group / Product Group or
  • a custom field on product

For example enforce the Wholesale rule with a Public Cloud validation in Custom Fields and Logic via Sales Document Check Before Save (SD_SLS_CHECK_BEFORE_SAVE) Badi. SAP explicitly supports these Cloud Badi for sales document checks in Public Cloud

More details below:

https://community.sap.com/t5/enterprise-resource-planning-blog-posts-by-sap/cloud-badis-to-check-sales-document-and-items/ba-p/14299492

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/2820950

Why this is recommended:

  • Meets both requirements exactly Wholesale restricted, Retail flexible
  • Avoids massive product exclusion/listing maintenance
  • Stays aligned with Public Cloud clean core design no attempt to bypass standard division rules

Option 2: Standard but data-heavy

If the customer insists that products must remain in divisions 10/20/30/40 for organizational/reporting reasons then the only standard way to allow a single Retail order is to ensure Retail-sellable items share one division. In practice that means creating Retail versions of materials /separate material numbers assigned to division 00

To reduce the maintenance effort in this scenario, use common distribution channels / common divisions reference sales areas to minimize repeated master-data maintenance across sales areas

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: SAP S/4HANA Cloud vs. Lightweight ERP for a Small Rental‑Driven Service Company
entry.content:
I’m currently in the middle of an ERP evaluation and would really appreciate some honest feedback from people who’ve seen similar setups in real life.

The company I’m looking at is small (around 25 employees), operates from two locations, and works in the traffic safety / temporary traffic management space. They provide services around construction sites and public space usage and rent out materials like traffic lights, signs and related equipment. All field work is done with their own vehicles and crews.

Right now, my main question is whether SAP S/4HANA Cloud, Public Edition is a good fit at all — or whether a lighter ERP (e.g. Odoo or a similar solution with strong rental support) would actually be the more honest recommendation.

What makes this tricky

Operationally, this is not a classic “sell, deliver, invoice” business.

  • Materials are rented out to projects, often for weeks or months
    They need to be clearly visible as available, reserved, in use, returned, sometimes partially.
  • Projects are non‑linear
    They get extended, paused, changed, and rarely follow a clean end‑to‑end flow.
  • Approvals and permits are long‑living states, not one‑time steps
    They create follow‑ups, reminders and work over time.
  • Tasks and reminders are derived from project and approval states, not created manually as standalone to‑dos.
  • The company runs its own fleet and crews and would like to:
    • Plan routes and daily assignments
    • Reflect what actually happens in the field
    • Feed that back into project status and billing

For now, the scale is still manageable — but the goal is to become more structured without over‑engineering.

Where I’m struggling with SAP Cloud ERP

From what I see so far, SAP S/4HANA Cloud feels very solid as a financial and transactional backbone.
But once I look at:

  • rental‑like material usage over time
  • dynamic task creation based on changing states
  • route and field crew planning

I start to wonder how much of this is realistically doable without adding:

  • SAP Field Service Management
  • additional service or asset modules
  • BTP extensions
  • or even third‑party tools

At that point, the solution stack starts to feel quite heavy for a company of this size.

The honest question I’m asking myself

Would it make more sense to recommend a lightweight ERP with strong rental and field service capabilities (like Odoo or a similar solution with rental depth), instead of pushing a cloud ERP that only really works once several extra products are added?

I’m not looking for a “SAP vs. non‑SAP” debate — I’m genuinely trying to understand:

  • What works well in standard SAP S/4HANA Cloud for small, operationally driven companies
  • Where the hard limits are
  • And where you personally would say: this is better handled outside of Cloud ERP

Any real‑world experience, lessons learned, or “don’t do this” advice would be very welcome.
I’m trying to avoid designing something that looks great on slides but creates friction in daily operations.

Thanks a lot in advance — looking forward to learning from your experiences.

entry.title: SAP HCM Payroll Processing & SAP SuccessFactors Employee Central Payroll - Discussions

entry.title: Meet Your HCM Learning Experts
entry.content:
The HCM Learning Group is supported by a team of SAP experts with many years of experience. We thought you might like to get to know them.

 

Tatiana Dymova

I'm a Product Learning Expert for SAP Payroll solutions based near Heidelberg, Germany. I specialize in helping learners master the full portfolio of SAP Payroll offerings, including SAP HCM Payroll for SAP ERP and SAP S/4HANA, SAP SuccessFactors Employee Central Payroll, Payroll Control Center (PCC), and SAP’s latest payroll innovation - SAP SuccessFactors Payroll. My role is twofold: I develop comprehensive learning content and deliver training sessions for global audiences. Outside of payroll, I’m passionate about traveling, discovering new places, attending theatre performances and concerts, and visiting exhibitions whenever I can. Always happy to connect with fellow professionals - let’s learn, innovate, and succeed together!

Jay Garcia

I’m an SAP Learning instructor for more than 10 years, focusing on SuccessFactors Employee Central's core features and functions, based in Manila, Philippines. My role helps me balance my need for solitude with my appreciation for interaction. Creating content for our courses allows me to enjoy some introspection and alone time, while teaching classes brings out my more social side. Lately, I’ve been rediscovering the joy of jigsaw puzzles and Sudoku.

Vicki Moening

Hello! I’m a Product Learning Specialist at SAP SuccessFactors. As an HR Professional, I've leveraged my extensive experience in HCM and consulting services to focus on client enablement and training, ensuring the successful configuration and implementation of systems. I specialize in Learning Management Systems, Compensation, and Variable Pay, offering training and developing curricula tailored to client needs. I'm always eager to learn new things, love traveling and discovering new cultures, and enjoy finding creative and fun ways to express myself.

Jordi Mosegui Thomas

I'm a product learning expert on the SAP SuccessFactors team, based in Spain. On my day-to-day, I deliver training and live sessions for Partners and Customers, to help them adopting SAP SuccessFactors, achieve their certification goals or get their SFX accreditation. I am passionate about the SAP SuccessFactors HCM Suite, AI capabilities, and my areas of expertise include Performance & Goals, Employee Central Core and Position Management, Talent Intelligence Hub, Platform and Career and Talent Development solution. I am also responsible for the content development of the learning journeys Employee Central Position Management, Performance & Goals and Platform Advanced and Talent Intelligence Hub. When I am not at work, I love travelling and exploring the world whenever I have the opportunity and learn about other cultures.

Kristin Steinweg

I am a highly experienced HR professional with more than 25 years of experience, bringing extensive process knowledge and expertise across all areas of human resources. As a trainer specializing in payroll within the SAP HCM on-premise environment, the SuccessFactors platform, and Employee Central, I deliver training to both beginners and advanced users, presenting complex topics in a clear, engaging, and structured manner. I place strong emphasis not only on explaining how processes are executed within SAP systems, but also on clearly conveying the underlying rationale behind them.

Lara Golden

As a Product Learning Expert at SAP, I specialize in learning content for SAP SuccessFactors Global Benefits, Compensation and Variable Pay. I joined SAP in 2018 following the acquisition of CallidusCloud and have been focused on creating great learning experiences ever since. You can find me at home on California’s Central Coast, where you'll usually find me walking on the beach or playing my guitar.

Alex White

Hello, community! I am a Senior Product Learning Specialist with a focus on SAP SuccessFactors Reporting Solutions, Employee Central, and Onboarding. I also develop learning content for SAP SuccessFactors. Since 2014, I have been committed to delivering high-quality training and learning materials for SAP, and I have over 25 years of experience teaching technical content. I am based in Knoxville, Tennessee, USA. Let’s learn and grow together.

entry.title: How to send email with attachments through SAP BTP Job Scheduler service
entry.content:
We have created action that the job scheduler triggers to send mail in CAPM and We are receiving email through SAP BTP Job Scheduler service without any attachments and getting success message.

When we attach any files and try to send the email using same action that the job scheduler triggers, we are getting success message and job scheduler log is also success, no code level error but email not received.

Created an application/service (CAP, Node.js)

This service will:

  • Generate or fetch the attachment (PDF, CSV, Excel, etc.) from Object Store on SAP BTP
  • Compose an email
  • Send the email using:
    • SAP BTP Destination (SMTP)
    • External mail provider (SendGrid)
Can SAP Job Scheduler send an attachment?

entry.title: How to filter a personalization attribute within mail creation in SAP Marketing Cloud
entry.content:
Hello SAP-community,

We have one question or challenge regarding missing filter options of a personalization attribute within mail creation.

Goal: We want to send mail campaigns to customers whose purchased products have a specific warranty date.

For this we replicate equipment data incl. of warranties from Sales Cloud to Marketing Cloud. A CBO (Custom Business Object) was designed to be able to use equipment details of accounts in Marketing campaigns.

Within the MC segmentation we are able to create the target group by filtering for all customers that purchased products in a specific timeframe. This segmentation works so far and – in my opinion – cannot fix our issue because this segmentation is just about targeting the correct customers.


As second step we create the mail content. As personalization attribute we use a table that shows the equipment of the corresponding customer, with two columns: Description and serial ID (which we both get from the CBO).

But here, we do not only get the products which we want to show because of their warranty date, we get every product the customer has purchased.

What we need is kind of filtering possibility for this table or an option that shows which product is affected by a specific warranty date.

Do you have an idea how to realize this?

Thank you and kind regards
Sören
